summary: Gruber dissects Steve Jobs's announcement that Apple will release an iPhone SDK in February 2008, analyzing what 'native third party applications' actually means. He argues the SDK will offer real Cocoa/UIKit development in Objective-C rather than Dashboard-style widgets, citing JavaScript performance benchmarks showing function calls are 226 times slower than compiled code. He explores Apple's likely security model, predicting a code-signing and sandboxing system similar to Nokia's approach, and speculates that app distribution will run through an iTunes Store model where Apple takes a cut of sales. The piece raises key questions about which developers will get access, whether apps will need Apple's case-by-case approval, and whether Apple will block apps like VOIP clients that threaten carrier revenue.
key_points: JavaScript performance on the iPhone is far too slow for native-quality apps, with function calls benchmarked at 226x slower than compiled Objective-C
Apple's security concerns are likely more about protecting carrier-sensitive internals like modem firmware than about external malware threats
Apple will probably require code signing tied to paid developer accounts, creating a Nokia-like trust model rather than a fully open platform
App distribution will likely flow through an iTunes Store model, giving Apple approval power and a revenue cut but potentially killing free third-party software
The 'everything runs as root' panic is overblown since even user-level exploits can access all personal data on any platform
money_quotes: Apple punditry is the Kremlinology of the tech world.
Third party developers want access to the same dog food Apple's own iPhone engineers are eating.
JavaScript on the current iPhone just isn't fast enough to provide an iPhone-caliber user experience.

I'm thinking Apple is more concerned about internal security — about having third-party apps limited to a sandbox so that user-installed code has no access to things like, say, the phone network modem's firmware.
Sure, root privileges allow an exploit to do anything, but the most important thing on your system is your personal data, and an exploit doesn't need root privileges to access that.
If you think the reason is laziness or ignorance on the part of the iPhone OS X engineers, you're an idiot.
key_insight: Apple's iPhone SDK delay is less about protecting users from malware and more about building a controlled ecosystem that protects carrier relationships and Apple's business interests through code signing, sandboxing, and centralized app distribution.
